items.question: Define machine learning in one sentence suitable for a non-technical stakeholder.
items.answer: Machine learning is a way to build software that learns patterns from historical examples so it can make predictions or decisions on new cases without someone writing a separate rule for every situation.

items.question: State Mitchell's definition of learning (E, T, P) and give a concrete example.
items.answer: A program learns from experience E with respect to task T and performance measure P if P improves with E. Example: E = labeled spam emails, T = classify inbox messages, P = classification accuracy—accuracy rises as more labeled mail is used for training.

items.question: What is the difference between training and inference?
items.answer: Training fits model parameters using a learning algorithm and training data (often by minimizing loss). Inference applies the trained, frozen model to new inputs to produce outputs without updating parameters.

items.question: Why can't you trust training-set accuracy alone?
items.answer: Training metrics are computed on data the model already saw while fitting parameters, so high accuracy may reflect memorization. Held-out validation or test data measures whether patterns generalize to unseen examples.

items.question: How does supervised learning relate to the ML definition?
items.answer: Supervised learning is ML where experience E consists of input-label pairs; the model learns a mapping from features to labels by minimizing prediction error on training examples, then predicts labels for new inputs at inference.
